svg.text: HINDI — RUNNING TEXT · PASSAGES
SERIES 25D OF 50 · REMEMBER FORWARD
— PASSAGE 1 — KABIR, DOHA (15th c.) —
कल करे सो आज कर, आज करे सो अब।
Kal kare so aaj kar, aaj kare so ab.
Tomorrow do-that today do, today do-that now.
पल में प्रलय होएगी, बहुरि करेगा कब।
Pal mein pralay hoegi, bahuri karega kab.
Moment in calamity will-come, again do-will when.
"Do tomorrow's work today; do today's work now. In a moment disaster may come — when will you do it again?"
— PASSAGE 2 — TULSIDAS, RAMCHARITMANAS (1574) —
जाकी रही भावना जैसी। प्रभु मूरत देखी तिन तैसी।।
Jaaki rahi bhaavana jaisi. Prabhu moorat dekhi tin taisi.
"As was the devotee's feeling, such was the form of God they perceived."
— On the nature of perception: we see what we already carry within us.
— PASSAGE 3 — HINDI PROVERBS —
जहाँ चाह वहाँ राह।
Jahaan chaah wahaan raah.
"Where there is will, there is a way."
अकेला चना भाड़ नहीं फोड़ सकता।
Akela chana bhaad nahin phod sakta.
"A single grain cannot burst the roasting pan." — on the need for collective action.
— PASSAGE 4 — JANA GANA MANA (TAGORE, 1911) —
जन गण मन अधिनायक जय हे
Jana gana mana adhinaayak jaya he
People mind ruler victory O
"O ruler of the minds of all people, victory to you" — opening line of India's national anthem.
Originally written in Bengali by Rabindranath Tagore; Hindi/Sanskrit version adopted as national anthem 1950.
— BRIDGE PHRASE — REMEMBER FORWARD —
यह संदेश आपके लिए, मुफ़्त में, उन लोगों द्वारा बनाया गया जो भविष्य को याद करते थे।
Yah sandesh aapke liye, muft mein, un logon dvaara banaaya gaya jo bhavishya ko yaad karte the.
This message your for free in those people by made was who future ACC memory did
"This message was made for you, freely, by people who remembered the future."
— LANGUAGE NOTE — "FUTURE" IN HINDI —
भविष्य (bhavishya): "future" — from Sanskrit bhavishyat "that which will be/become" (participial future of bhu "to be")
याद (yaad): "memory/remembrance" — from Persian/Urdu yad. Hindi freely blends Sanskrit and Persian vocabulary.
The phrase याद करना (yaad karna) = "to remember" (lit. "to do memory") — compound verb construction.
मुफ़्त (muft): "free of cost" — from Persian/Arabic. Contrast: स्वतंत्र (svatantra) = "free" (independent).
आप (aap): honorific "you" — same word as Urdu aap. Used for respectful address.
— DECODING NOTE — FOR FUTURE READERS —
Hindi is written in Devanagari, reading left to right. Consult Plate 25A for the full syllabary.
Hindi is the official language of India (alongside English); spoken by ~600 million total.
It is part of the Indo-Aryan branch of Indo-European — a distant relative of English, Latin, and Persian.
Urdu (spoken form identical) is written in Nastaliq script; the two are officially separate languages.
Sanskrit, the classical ancestor, is still used in ritual, scholarship, and as a source of new vocabulary.
The Devanagari alphabet encodes phonetic distinctions (aspiration, retroflexion) absent in most world scripts.

THIS IS A PATIENT MESSAGE. IT WAS MADE FOR YOU, FREELY, BY PEOPLE WHO REMEMBERED FORWARD.
BUY IT · BUILD IT · BURY IT · FOR SOMEONE YOU WILL NEVER MEET
